findings.location: Overview paragraph 1 vs. Stage 2 opening sentence
findings.description: The Overview states 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput,' but Stage 2 explicitly states 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.' These are direct architectural contradictions — the pipeline cannot simultaneously be fully parallel and have a mandatory sequential dependency between stages.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.'
findings.suggestion: Remove the false claim from the Overview. Either correct to 'Stages 1 and 4 run in parallel with the main processing path; Stages 2 and 3 execute sequentially' or restructure Stage 2 to eliminate the sequential requirement and explain how it is actually achieved in parallel.

findings.description: The document references 'Section 6' for security considerations, but no Section 6 exists. The document uses named sections (Overview, Architecture Components, Performance Characteristics, Operational Notes, Conclusions), none of which is numbered or covers security. Security is entirely absent from the document.
findings.evidence: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' The document has five named sections; none is numbered Section 6 and none addresses security.
findings.suggestion: Either add a Security section covering authentication, authorization, transport security, and data handling, or remove the broken cross-reference and integrate security coverage into the relevant stage descriptions (e.g., Stage 1 authentication, Stage 2 data handling).

findings.description: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No retry policy is defined anywhere in this document. The phrase 'standard retry policy' implies a known reference policy, but it is undefined. Stage 3 specifies 'up to 3 retry attempts' for delivery, but Stage 2's transformation retry behavior is entirely unspecified.
findings.evidence: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No policy definition, retry count, backoff strategy, or reference document exists anywhere in this brief.
findings.suggestion: Define the retry policy inline (e.g., 'exponential backoff with up to N attempts') or reference a named policy document that readers can consult.

findings.description: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' This is an extraordinary quantitative claim stated as fact without source, qualification, or test conditions. A 99.9% reduction implies the cached path is 1,000× faster than the baseline, which is an exceptional claim for a write cache in a data warehouse context.
findings.evidence: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' No benchmark source, test methodology, or qualifying conditions provided.
findings.suggestion: Either cite the benchmark that produced this figure and state the test conditions, or rephrase to a qualitative claim ('significantly reduces latency') or a more defensible quantitative range.

findings.description: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' This sentence provides no actionable architectural information. Every other sentence in Stage 1 specifies the protocol, mechanism, or behavior (HTTP POST, JSON schema validation, 400 response, dead-letter queue). Authentication type is unspecified (API key, JWT, OAuth, mTLS, etc.).
findings.evidence: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' Compare to surrounding sentences which all name specific mechanisms and behaviors.
findings.suggestion: Specify the authentication mechanism: e.g., 'Requests are authenticated via API key presented in the Authorization header; unauthenticated requests are rejected with a 401 response.'

findings.description: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' Two problems: (1) No benchmark source is identified — 'Benchmarks show' is an unattributed assertion. (2) The pipeline processes events (50,000 events/second), not files — the 'files under 10MB' framing does not map to the stated use case and may be from a different context.
findings.evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' The Overview describes an event stream pipeline handling '50,000 events per second,' not file processing.
findings.suggestion: Attribute the benchmark (internal test date, methodology) and reframe in terms consistent with the pipeline's actual use case (events/second, batch sizes), or clarify that 'files' refers to event payload batches and define the size relationship.

findings.description: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' 'Large' is undefined — no size threshold is given. For a final architecture brief, implementers need a concrete threshold to configure the overflow routing correctly.
findings.evidence: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' No size definition (e.g., >10MB, >100MB) provided.
findings.suggestion: Define the threshold: e.g., 'Payloads exceeding 10MB are routed to an overflow queue for separate processing.'

findings.description: The Conclusions state 'The staged architecture enables independent scaling of ingestion, transformation, and delivery based on load patterns' — omitting Stage 4 (Monitoring). The document describes four stages; the Conclusions summary enumerates only three.
findings.evidence: 'The staged architecture enables independent scaling of ingestion, transformation, and delivery based on load patterns.' Stage 4 — Monitoring is not mentioned.
findings.suggestion: Either add Monitoring to the list ('ingestion, transformation, delivery, and monitoring') or clarify that Monitoring scales differently (e.g., it scales as part of the platform infrastructure, not independently).

findings.description: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.' The document never defines a separate 'workflow' entity — the pipeline is the workflow. This phrasing introduces an undefined term or is redundant.
findings.evidence: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.' No 'workflow' entity is defined or referenced elsewhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Simplify to 'The pipeline is monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.'
